section.p: Select and enter any of these types of adjustments to income you had in . Do not enter the same adjustment in multiple places.
Enter up to of the interest you paid on your qualified student loan. You can find this on your Form 1098-E if you paid more than $600 in interest.
The loan must have paid qualified higher education expenses to attend an eligible educational institution. Also, the expenses must have been:

ul.li: For you, your spouse, or a person who was your dependent when you took out the loan;
Paid or incurred within a reasonable period of time before or after you took out the loan; and
For education provided during an academic period for an eligible student.

section.p: You can't claim the deduction if you aren't legally obligated to make payments on the loan.
For more information, see Publication 970, Tax Benefits for Education (opens in a new tab).

fg-set.question: Student loan interest you you and your spouse paid in

section.p: If you're a teacher, enter up to ( if married filing jointly and both you and your spouse are teachers) of unreimbursed expenses such as:

ul.li: Fees for participation in professional development courses
Books and supplies
Computer equipment (including related software and services)
Other equipment
Supplementary materials that you use in the classroom

section.p: For more information, see IRS Topic 458 (opens in a new tab).

fg-set.question: Educator expenses youyou and your spouse paid in

section.h3: Deduction for contributions to an IRA that is not included in payroll deductions
section.p: Enter any IRA contributions you made outside of your payroll contributions.

p.strong: Don't include amounts that you included above.

section.p: For more information see, IRA Deduction Limits (opens in a new tab).

fg-set.question: Traditional (pre-tax) IRA contributions youyou and your spouse made in

section.p: If youyou or your spouse were self employed and made contributions to qualified retirement plans in , you may have been eligible for the self-employed retirement plan deduction.
To qualify, youyou or your spouse must have:

ul.li: Been a sole proprietor
Been a working partner in a partnership, or
Had a limited liability company

section.p: Eligible plans may have included:

ul.li: Simplified Employee Pension (SEP) plan
Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees
Solo-401(k)
Other qualified retirement plans

section.p: Don't include Individual Retirement Arrangement (IRA) contributions.
You must have used a special rule to calculate retirement plan contributions for yourself.

p.a: Learn more about self-employed retirement contributions. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Self-employed retirement contributions youyou and your spouse made in

section.h3: Health Savings Account (HSA) deductions that are not included in payroll deductions
section.p: Enter any Health Savings Account (HSA) contributions that you didn't include under HSA, FSA, or other “cafeteria plan” above.
For more information visit About Publication 969, Health Savings Accounts and Other Tax-Favored Health Plans (opens in a new tab).

fg-set.question: HSA contributions youyou or your spouse made in

section.p: If you made alimony or separate maintenance payments under a court order that was executed before January 1, 2019 and the order didn't specifically state that you can't deduct your payments, enter the amount you paid during year here.
Don't include amounts you paid for:
Also don't include amounts if the court order was modified and states that you can't deduct your payments.
Alimony or separate maintenance made under a court order executed after December 31, 2024
For more information, see Tax Topic 452 (opens in a new tab).
section.h3: Penalty for Early Withdrawal of Savings
section.p: If you withdraw funds from a certificate of deposit or other deferred interest account before maturity, you may be charged a penalty. You can find this penalty on your Form 1099-INT in box 2. Enter that amount here.

fg-set.question: Penalty amount youyou and your spouse paid in

section.p: If you are a member of the armed forces on active duty, and the military ordered you to permanently change your duty station, enter any moving expenses the military didn't reimburse you for here. Moving expenses include:

ul.li: Direct travel from your old home to your new one
Cost of packing and moving your personal belongings

section.p: For more information, see Tax Topic 455 (opens in a new tab).

fg-set.question: Moving expenses youyou and your spouse paid in

section.p: If you are a/an:

ul.li: Armed Forces reservist
Qualified performing artist
Fee-basis state or local government official
Employee with impairment-related work expenses

section.p: You may include expenses that are common and accepted in your field of trade, business, or profession, or expenses for things that are helpful and appropriate for your business.
If you don't fit into one of the listed categories, you may not claim adjustments for these expenses.
For more information and a list of qualified expenses, see Form 2106, Employee Business Expenses (opens in a new tab).

fg-set.question: Eligible business expenses youyou and your spouse paid in

section.p: If you wereyou or your spouse were self employed and had a net profit for the year, you could have deducted your health insurance premiums.

Eligible premiums were medical, dental, vision, and long-term care insurance. The policy could be for yourself, your spouse, and your dependents. The policy could also cover your child who was under the age of 27 at the end of the year, even if the child wasn't your dependent.

p.a: Learn more about self-employed health insurance. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Health insurance premiums youyou and your spouse paid in
Other adjustments youyou and your spouse paid in

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: Based on your adjusted gross income (including the adjustments you entered), you don’t qualify for the EITC for .
For more information, see Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.

modal-content.p: Adjustments are expenses, payments, contributions, and fees that you may have been able to subtract from your total income to reduce how much of your income got taxed. They are a type of deduction.
